mapQuestions.question: Which TWO continents is Turkey on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe AND Asia. Turkey is one of only a few countries that sit on more than one continent. Istanbul, the biggest city, is split between the two by the Bosphorus strait 🌍🌏

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Turkey?
mapQuestions.answer: Ankara, in the centre of the country. Istanbul is bigger and more famous but it is not the capital.

mapQuestions.question: What seas touch Turkey?
mapQuestions.answer: Four: the Black Sea (north), the Mediterranean (south), the Aegean (west), and the Sea of Marmara (which Istanbul sits on).

mapQuestions.question: What is the highest mountain in Turkey?
mapQuestions.answer: Mount Ararat (Ağrı Dağı), 5,137 metres, in the far east. In old stories, this is where Noah's Ark was said to have landed.

mapQuestions.question: What is the Bosphorus?
mapQuestions.answer: A narrow strip of sea, only about 700m wide at its narrowest, that runs through the middle of Istanbul. It divides Europe from Asia, and connects the Black Sea to the Mediterranean.

phrases.english: Goodbye (said by person leaving / person staying)

foods.name: Kebab
foods.description: Not one dish but a whole family. Adana (spicy minced lamb on a sword skewer), shish (cubed lamb), iskender (sliced doner over bread, butter and yoghurt). Every region has its own.

foods.name: Baklava
foods.description: Thin layers of buttery pastry stacked 30-40 high, filled with crushed pistachios or walnuts, baked, then soaked in honey or sugar syrup. The most famous Turkish dessert. Gaziantep does it best.

foods.name: Kahvaltı (Turkish breakfast)
foods.description: A whole table covered in 15+ small plates: olives, white cheese, cucumber, tomato, jam, honey, eggs, bread, butter, sausage, soft-boiled eggs. Taken seriously, eaten slowly with tea.

foods.name: Köfte
foods.description: Turkish meatballs, grilled or pan-fried. Spiced with cumin and parsley. Served with rice or bread, salad and yoghurt. Every region has a slightly different recipe.

foods.name: Pide
foods.description: A boat-shaped flatbread topped with cheese, meat or eggs and baked in a wood-fired oven. Sometimes called "Turkish pizza", but pide is older and has a different history.

foods.name: Çay (Turkish tea)
foods.description: Strong black tea brewed in a double-stacked teapot and served in tiny tulip-shaped glasses. Turks drink it from breakfast to bedtime, on every social occasion, and as a sign of welcome.

scavengerItems.label: A street cat (Istanbul has tens of thousands of them, all friendly, all looked after by the neighbourhood)
A "nazar" evil-eye bead hanging in a shop, taxi, doorway or as jewellery
A mosque with pencil-thin minarets (the call to prayer happens 5 times a day)
Someone walking with a tray of tulip glasses of çay balanced on it
Hot-air balloons rising at dawn over Cappadocia (or pictures of them)
Long strings of dried red chillies or aubergines hanging from balconies
A "döner" rotating spit of meat outside a kebab shop
Fishermen lined up shoulder to shoulder on the Galata Bridge in Istanbul
A "hamam" (Turkish bath) sign with a domed roof and steaming chimney
A "tuğra" (sultan's royal signature) on an old mosque or palace wall
A pattern of "iznik tiles" (blue and white floral patterns) on a wall or fountain
A whirling dervish (especially in Konya) or a picture of one

geoMatches.description: A city on two continents. The old Byzantine capital became the Ottoman capital, and is now Turkey's biggest city. Hagia Sophia, the Blue Mosque, the Grand Bazaar.
Central Anatolia. Strange volcanic rock formations called "fairy chimneys", entire underground cities, and hundreds of hot-air balloons rising at sunrise.
On the Aegean coast. The ruins of one of the great cities of the ancient world, with a perfectly preserved 25,000-seat theatre and the marble facade of an ancient library.
"Cotton Castle". Brilliant white terraces of mineral pools cascading down a hillside in the south-west, with the Roman ruins of Hierapolis on top.
On the Mediterranean (the "Turquoise Coast"). Long golden beaches, tall Taurus mountains behind, and well-preserved Roman ruins scattered along the coast.
The wet, green northern coast, very different from the dry rest of Turkey. Tea plantations, hazelnut farms, dramatic cliffs and steady rain.

senses.smell: Roasted lamb and spices, fresh bread from a bakery at dawn, jasmine in the alleys, hot dust on stone steps, Black Sea pine forests, hot apple tea, simit (sesame bread)…
senses.hear: The call to prayer ("ezan") five times a day from every minaret, "Çay! Çay!" shouted by tea sellers, ships horns on the Bosphorus, cats meowing in unison, accordion music…
senses.taste: Strong sweet black tea, smoky grilled kebab, sticky pistachio baklava, salty white cheese, fresh tomatoes that actually taste like tomatoes, the punch of fresh herbs…
senses.feel: Cool marble in a hamam, hot dry summer wind, the soft crunch of simit bread, warm Mediterranean water, the rumble of an Istanbul ferry crossing the Bosphorus…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is Istanbul ruled by cats?
stories.body: Istanbul has somewhere between 100,000 and 750,000 stray cats. Nobody knows the exact number. They are everywhere: curled up on car bonnets, lounging in mosques (even on the carpet during prayers), sleeping inside shoes outside shops, swatting fish from the harbour. But nothing about it feels gross or sad, because the whole city looks after them. Locals leave out food and water, vets treat them for free, shopkeepers know each one by name, and the police chief once said cats were welcome at his station. The cat culture goes back hundreds of years. The Prophet Muhammad was said to have loved cats, which helped, and during Ottoman times cats kept the city free of rats. Today they are basically Istanbul's unofficial mascots.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If your town adopted one type of animal as its unofficial mascot, free to roam, fed by everyone, what should it be?

stories.question: Why are there hundreds of hot-air balloons in one place at sunrise?
stories.body: In Cappadocia, in central Turkey, soft volcanic rock has been worn by wind and water into a moonscape of pointy "fairy chimneys", canyons and underground cities (some big enough to hide 20,000 people, dug by early Christians 1,500 years ago). The landscape is so otherworldly that hot-air balloons fly over it every morning, weather permitting. At dawn, around 150 huge colourful balloons rise silently together, drifting between rocky valleys as the sun comes up. It is one of the most photographed sights in the world. Pilots have to navigate carefully because the wind through the valleys can be tricky, but most days it is calm.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Would you go up in a hot-air balloon at sunrise? Why is going somewhere at sunrise different from going there at midday?

stories.question: Why is a Turkish bath a whole afternoon?
stories.body: A "hamam" is a traditional Turkish bath, in a domed marble building usually next door to a mosque. The whole thing takes a couple of hours. You start in a hot steam room, where you sweat for a while on a heated marble slab. Then a "tellak" (bath attendant) scrubs every inch of your skin with a rough mitt that takes off dead skin in long grey rolls (slightly disgusting, very satisfying). Then they wash you with mountains of soapy bubbles. Then you sit in the warm, sleepy main hall drinking tea or fresh juice. The tradition is over 600 years old. Most Turkish neighbourhoods used to have one. Many still do.

stories.question: Why has one Istanbul building been a church, a mosque, a museum and a mosque again?
stories.body: Hagia Sophia stands on a hill in central Istanbul. Built in the year 537 by the Byzantine emperor Justinian, it was, for almost a thousand years, the biggest church in the Christian world. When the Ottoman Turks took the city in 1453, they converted it into a mosque, adding four minarets and plastering over the Christian mosaics. In 1934, the modern Turkish leader Atatürk made it a museum, uncovering many of the original mosaics, so that both religions could appreciate it. In 2020, the Turkish government turned it back into a working mosque. Today it is both: a functioning mosque with millions of pilgrims, AND a place where you can still see 1,500-year-old mosaics of Mary and Jesus on the walls.
stories.thinkingQuestion: When a building has had so many uses over hundreds of years, who do you think it really "belongs" to?

stories.question: Who are the whirling dervishes?
stories.body: In the 13th century, a poet and Sufi mystic called Rumi lived in Konya in central Turkey. Sufism is a mystical branch of Islam that believes you can experience the presence of God through music, poetry and movement. After Rumi died in 1273, his followers developed a slow turning meditation, the "sema", in which dancers spin on their right foot with their right hand pointed up to receive divine love and their left hand pointed down to give it to the world. Their white skirts rise into perfect circles. They wear tall brown felt hats which are said to represent the tombstone of the ego (the showy self), which the dancer is letting go of. Watching a real sema is hypnotic, slow, calm, and not at all like a performance.

convoQuestions.question: What's the best Turkish thing you've eaten so far?
How many street cats have you said hello to today?
Have you tried Turkish breakfast? Whose plate had the most things on it?
What does it feel like to be in a city that is half in Europe and half in Asia?
If you could spend tomorrow in a balloon over Cappadocia or in a hamam in Istanbul, which?
Turkey sits between Europe, Asia, the old Soviet Union and the Middle East. How do you think being in the middle of all of that shapes a country?

wordSearch.hint: Turkish foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Istanbul street cat
animals.description: Istanbul is famous for its cats. They have their own documentaries and food bowls everywhere.

animals.name: Friendly street dog
animals.description: Tagged and vaccinated by the city. Big, calm, used to people.

animals.name: Feral pigeon
animals.description: New Mosque's courtyard in Eminönü is famous for the pigeon flocks.

animals.name: White stork
animals.description: On rooftops along the Aegean coast and in Cappadocia, especially in spring.

animals.name: Angora goat
animals.description: Long silky white hair — the source of mohair wool, from the Ankara region.

animals.name: Loggerhead sea turtle
animals.description: Nests on Iztuzu Beach in Dalyan — protected by guards every summer.
